subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the INCORRECT statements from the following:
A. Notation ${ }_{12}^{24} \mathrm{Mg}$ represents 24 protons and 12 neutrons.
B. Wavelength of a radiation of frequency $4.5 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ is $6.7 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m}$.
C. One radiation has wavelength $=\lambda_{1}(900 \mathrm{~nm})$ and energy $=\mathrm{E}_{1}$. Other radiation has wavelength $=\lambda_{2}(300 \mathrm{~nm})$ and energy $=\mathrm{E}_{2}. \mathrm{E}_{1}: \mathrm{E}_{2}=3: 1$.
D. Number of photons of light of wavelength 2000 pm that provides 1 J of energy is $1.006 \times 10^{16}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Identify the INCORRECT statements from the following:
A. Notation ${ }_{12}^{24} \mathrm{Mg}$ represents 24 protons and 12 neutrons.
B. Wavelength of a radiation of frequency $4.5 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ is $6.7 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m}$.
C. One radiation has wavelength $=\lambda_{1}(900 \mathrm{~nm})$ and energy $=\mathrm{E}_{1}$. Other radiation has wavelength $=\lambda_{2}(300 \mathrm{~nm})$ and energy $=\mathrm{E}_{2}. \mathrm{E}_{1}: \mathrm{E}_{2}=3: 1$.
D. Number of photons of light of wavelength 2000 pm that provides 1 J of energy is $1.006 \times 10^{16}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Checking each statement:
(A) states $_{12}^{24}Mg$ has 24 protons, but the mass number is 24 and atomic number is 12, so this is INCORRECT.
(B) Using $\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4.5 \times 10^{15}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-8}$ m, which is CORRECT.
(C) For $\lambda_1 = 900$ nm and $\lambda_2 = 300$ nm, since energy is inversely proportional to wavelength, $E_1:E_2 = 1:3$, not $3:1$ as stated, so this is INCORRECT.
(D) For photons at $\lambda = 2000$ pm, energy per photon is $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = 9.94 \times 10^{-17}$ J, giving $N = 1.006 \times 10^{16}$ photons for 1 J, which is CORRECT.
The incorrect statements are A and C.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Rutherford's gold foil experiment cannot explain the line spectrum of hydrogen atom. Statement II : Bohr's model of hydrogen atom contradicts Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Rutherford's gold foil experiment cannot explain the line spectrum of hydrogen atom. Statement II : Bohr's model of hydrogen atom contradicts Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true.
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false.
Both statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false.
Both statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true.
solution:
Rutherford's gold foil experiment only proved that electrons are held towards nucleus by electrostatic forces of attraction and move in circular orbits with very high speeds.

Bohr's model gave exact formula for simultaneous calculation of speed & distance of electron from the nucleus, something which was deemed impossible according to Heisenberg.

subject: Chemistry
q: A gas absorbs a photon of $355 \mathrm{~nm}$ and emits at two wavelengths. If one of the emissions is at $680 \mathrm{~nm}$, the other is at:
question: A gas absorbs a photon of $355 \mathrm{~nm}$ and emits at two wavelengths. If one of the emissions is at $680 \mathrm{~nm}$, the other is at:
solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {absorted }}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{355}=\frac{1}{680}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda_2=742.8 \cong 743 \mathrm{~nm}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following pairs of electrons

(A) (a) n=3, l=1, ml=1,ms=+12
(b) n=3, l=2,ml=1,ms=+12
(B) (a) n=3, l=2,ml=-2,ms=-12
(b) n=3, l=2,ml=-1,ms=-12
(C) (a) n=4, l=2,ml=2,ms=+12
(b) n=3,l=2,ml=2,ms=+12

The pairs of electrons present in degenerate orbitals is/are
question:
Consider the following pairs of electrons

(A) (a) n=3, l=1, ml=1,ms=+12
(b) n=3, l=2,ml=1,ms=+12
(B) (a) n=3, l=2,ml=-2,ms=-12
(b) n=3, l=2,ml=-1,ms=-12
(C) (a) n=4, l=2,ml=2,ms=+12
(b) n=3,l=2,ml=2,ms=+12

The pairs of electrons present in degenerate orbitals is/are
solution:
The orbitals which have same energy are called degenerate orbitals. Energy or an orbital depends on two quantum numbers, principle quantum number (n) and azimuthal quantum number (l).

The orbitals with similar values of n & l but with different value of m will be degenerate orbitals.

Among the given set or orbital only option (B) fulfil this condition.

subject: Chemistry
q: Ejection of the photoelectron from metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$ when the radiation of $250 \mathrm{~nm}$ is used. The work function of the metal is :
question: Ejection of the photoelectron from metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$ when the radiation of $250 \mathrm{~nm}$ is used. The work function of the metal is :
solution:
$\lambda=250 \mathrm{~nm}=2500 Å$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{12400}{2500}=4.96 \mathrm{~eV}$
$\mathrm{KE}=$ stopping potential $=0.5 \mathrm{~eV}$
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{W}_0+\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}$.
$4.96=\mathrm{W}_0+0.5$
$\mathrm{~W}_0=4.46 \approx 4.5 \mathrm{~eV}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statements are correct, if the threshold frequency of caesium is $5.16 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ ?
A. When Cs is placed inside a vacuum chamber with an ammeter connected to it and yellow light is focused on Cs the ammeter shows the presence of current.
B. When the brightness of the yellow light is dimmed, the value of the current in the ammeter is reduced.
C. When a red light is used instead to the yellow light, the current produced is higher with respect to the yellow light.
D. When a blue light is used, the ammeter shows the formation of current.
E. When a white light is used, the ammeter shows formation of current.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Which of the following statements are correct, if the threshold frequency of caesium is $5.16 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ ?
A. When Cs is placed inside a vacuum chamber with an ammeter connected to it and yellow light is focused on Cs the ammeter shows the presence of current.
B. When the brightness of the yellow light is dimmed, the value of the current in the ammeter is reduced.
C. When a red light is used instead to the yellow light, the current produced is higher with respect to the yellow light.
D. When a blue light is used, the ammeter shows the formation of current.
E. When a white light is used, the ammeter shows formation of current.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: A, C, D and E Only
A, B, D and E Only
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{3 \times 10}{5.16 \times 10^{14}} \\ & \lambda=581.39 \mathrm{~nm}\end{aligned}$
* $\lambda_{\text {Photon }}$ is near $\&$ below yellow light it can show photoelectric effect.
* If intensity of light decreases photocurrent decreases.
* Red light will not produce photoelectric effect.
* $v_{\text {Blue }} \gt v_{\text {yellow }}$ so photoelectric current will be produced.
* White light contain all frequencies so it will show photo electric current.
Correct statements are $\mathrm{~A~B~D~} \& \mathrm{~E}$.

q: The shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The longest wavelength in Balmer series of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> is
question: The shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The longest wavelength in Balmer series of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> is

From (1) & (2)

λHe+λH=95

λHe+=λ×95

λHe+=9λ5.

subject: Chemistry
q: The wavelength of an electron of kinetic energy 4.50×10-29 J is ________×10–5 m. (Nearest integer) Given: mass of electron is9×10–31 kg, h=6.6×10–34 Js
question: The wavelength of an electron of kinetic energy 4.50×10-29 J is ________×10–5 m. (Nearest integer) Given: mass of electron is9×10–31 kg, h=6.6×10–34 Js
solution:
Given:
Kinetic energy(K.E.)=4.55×10−29J
Mass of electron(me)=9.1×10−31kgh=6.6×10−34J−sec

debroglie wavelength λd=hmv=h2mKE

Here λ is the de Broglie wavelength, h is Planck's constant, m is the mass of the particle, v is its velocity, and p is the momentum of the particle, which is equal to mv.

λ=h2mKE

=6.6×10-342×9×10-31×4.5×10-29=6.6×10-349×10-30=6.69×10-4

=669×10-5

=7.33×10-5

q:
The incorrect postulates of the Dalton's atomic theory are :
(A) Atoms of different elements differ in mass.
(B) Matter consists of divisible atoms.
(C) Compounds are formed when atoms of different element combine in a fixed ratio.
(D) All the atoms of given element have different properties including mass.
(E) Chemical reactions involve reorganisation of atoms.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:

(A) Atoms of different elements differ in mass.
(B) Matter consists of divisible atoms.
(C) Compounds are formed when atoms of different element combine in a fixed ratio.
(D) All the atoms of given element have different properties including mass.
(E) Chemical reactions involve reorganisation of atoms.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution: $B, D$

question: The correct set of four quantum numbers for the valence electrons of rubidium atom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>37</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
The electronic configuration can be written as,

1s2, 2s2, 2p6, 3s2, 3p6, 4s2, 3d10, 4p6, 5s1

The outermost electron has i.e., Kr365s1

Principal quantum number=5

Azimuthal quantum number=0 (corresponds to s)

Magnetic quantum number=0

Spin quantum number = + 1 2 (positive spin)

subject: Chemistry
q: If $m$ and $e$ are the mass and charge of the revolving electron in the orbit of radius $r$ for hydrogen atom, the total energy of the revolving electron will be:
question: If $m$ and $e$ are the mass and charge of the revolving electron in the orbit of radius $r$ for hydrogen atom, the total energy of the revolving electron will be:
solution:
Total energy of a revolving electron is the sum of its kinetic and potential energy. Total energy $=$ K.E. $+$ P. E.
$$
=\frac{e^2}{2 r}+\left(-\frac{e^2}{r}\right)=-\frac{e^2}{2 r}
$$

q: If wavelength of the first line of the Paschen series of hydrogen atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>720</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>, then the wavelength of the second line of this series is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: If wavelength of the first line of the Paschen series of hydrogen atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>720</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>, then the wavelength of the second line of this series is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
Paschen series are spectral lines coming to n=3

1st line of Paschen series makes a transition from, 4→3

So, 2nd line of Paschen series makes a transition from, 5→3

We know that,

1λ=R1n12-1n22Z2

For 1st line, 1λ=R1(3)2-1(4)2Z2

and for 2nd line,

1λ'=R1(3)2-1(5)2Z2

or 1λ1λ'=R79×16Z2R169×25Z2

Given, λ=720 nm

or 17201λ'=175256

or  λ'720=175256

or λ' ≈ 492 nm

subject: Chemistry
q: For hydrogen atom, energy of an electron in first excited state is \(-3.4 \mathrm{eV}, \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}\). of the same electron of hydrogen atom is \(x \mathrm{eV}\). Value of \(x\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{eV}\). (Nearest integer)
question: For hydrogen atom, energy of an electron in first excited state is \(-3.4 \mathrm{eV}, \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}\). of the same electron of hydrogen atom is \(x \mathrm{eV}\). Value of \(x\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{eV}\). (Nearest integer)
solution:
Kinetic energy of electron is, 
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{13 \cdot 6 \mathrm{Z}^2}{\mathrm{n}^2} \mathrm{eV}$ 
For the first excited state of the hydrogen atom, 
$\mathrm{n}=2$ and $\mathrm{Z}=1$ $\therefore \mathrm{KE}=\frac{13.6}{2^2}=3.4 \mathrm{eV}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
If the wavelength for an electron emitted from H- atom is 3.3×10-10 m, then energy absorbed by the electron in its ground state compared to minimum energy required for its escape from the atom, is____times.
[Given : h=6.626×10-34Js, Mass of electron =9.1×10-31]
question:
If the wavelength for an electron emitted from H- atom is 3.3×10-10 m, then energy absorbed by the electron in its ground state compared to minimum energy required for its escape from the atom, is____times.
[Given : h=6.626×10-34Js, Mass of electron =9.1×10-31]
solution:
De Broglie's equation for calculating the wave length(λ) associated with the electron is given below

λ=hp=hmV

Let Kinetic energy of the particle of mass 'm' is K

K=12mV22Km=m2V22Km=mV

λ=h2mK

h=Planck's constant=6.62×10-27erg-sec

K=h22 mλ2

K=h22 mλ2=43.9×10-682×9.1×10-31×10.89×10-20

K=2.215×10-18

Eabs=Ereq+KEabs=Energy absorbedEreq=Energy requiredK=Kinetic energyEabsEreq=1+KEreq=1+2.215×10-1813.6×1.602×10-19=2.0166

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is correct set of $4$ quantum numbers of $19^{th}$ electron in Chromium (Atomic number $= 24$) in accordance with Aufbau principle?
question: Which of the following is correct set of $4$ quantum numbers of $19^{th}$ electron in Chromium (Atomic number $= 24$) in accordance with Aufbau principle?
solution:
The atomic number of Chromium is $24$.

According to the Aufbau principle, the order of filling of electrons is $1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d$.

The electronic configuration of the first $18$ electrons is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6$.

The $19^{th}$ electron enters the $4s$ orbital.

For the $4s$ orbital, the quantum numbers are:
Principal quantum number, $n = 4$
Azimuthal quantum number, $l = 0$
Magnetic quantum number, $m = 0$
Spin quantum number, $s = +\dfrac{1}{2}$ or $-\dfrac{1}{2}$

This matches the set given in the fourth option.

Answer: $n = 4, l = 0, m = 0, s = +\dfrac{1}{2}$

question: Which one of the following constitutes a group of the isoelectronic species?

q: The energy of an electron in first Bohr's orbit of H atom is <math> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>13.6</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>eV</mtext><mtext>.</mtext></mrow> </math> The energy value of electron in the first excited state of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mtext>Li</mtext></mrow> <mrow> <mtext>2+</mtext></mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> is :
question: The energy of an electron in first Bohr's orbit of H atom is <math> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>13.6</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>eV</mtext><mtext>.</mtext></mrow> </math> The energy value of electron in the first excited state of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mtext>Li</mtext></mrow> <mrow> <mtext>2+</mtext></mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> is :
solution:
For H atom & H like species
The electronic energy in the nth orbit is

E n = - 13.6 z 2 n 2 eV

E n Li 2 + = - 13.6 × 9 n 2 eV = - 122.4 eV n 2

If n = 2

E 2 Li 2 + = - 122.4 2 2 = - 30.6 eV

subject: Chemistry
q: The energy required by electrons, present in the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom to be excited to second Bohr orbit is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. Given: $R_{H}=2.18 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{ergs}$.
question: The energy required by electrons, present in the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom to be excited to second Bohr orbit is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. Given: $R_{H}=2.18 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{ergs}$.
solution:
The energy of an electron in the $n^{th}$ Bohr orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $E_n = -R_H \left( \frac{1}{n^2} \right)$.
The energy required for excitation from $n=1$ to $n=2$ for a single atom is $\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = R_H \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2} \right) = R_H \left( 1 - \frac{1}{4} \right) = \frac{3}{4} R_H$.
Given $R_H = 2.18 \times 10^{-11}$ ergs.
Converting ergs to Joules: $1 \text{ erg} = 10^{-7} \text{ J}$.
So, $R_H = 2.18 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{-7} = 2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$.
The energy required per atom is $\Delta E = \frac{3}{4} \times 2.18 \times 10^{-18} = 1.635 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$.
To find the energy required per mole of electrons, multiply by Avogadro's number $N_A \approx 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$.
Energy per mole $= 1.635 \times 10^{-18} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ J mol}^{-1}$.
Energy per mole $\approx 9.846 \times 10^5 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$.
Comparing with the given options, $9.835 \times 10^5$ is the closest value.

subject: Chemistry
q: The figure that is not a direct manifestation of the quantum nature of atom is :
question: The figure that is not a direct manifestation of the quantum nature of atom is :
solution:
1, 2 and 3 are according to quantum theory but (4) is statement of kinetic theory of gases.

Figure 1 is associated with theory of black body, figure 2 is associated with photoelectric effect and figure 3 shows absorption spectra, so we can say that these 3 figures are associated with quantum nature of atom while figure 4 shows variation of internal energy with temperature .

question:
For the Balmer series, in the spectrum of H atom, v-=RH1n12-1n22, the correct statements among I to IV are,
I As wavelength decreases, the lines in the series converge.
II The integer n1 is equal to 2/
III The lines of the longest wavelength correspond to n2=3.
IV The ionization energy of hydrogen can be calculated from the wave number of these lines.

subject: Chemistry
q: The de-Broglie's wavelength of electron present in first Bohr orbit of ' $\mathrm{H}$ ' atom is:
question: The de-Broglie's wavelength of electron present in first Bohr orbit of ' $\mathrm{H}$ ' atom is:
solution:
First Bohr orbit of $\mathrm{H}$ atom has radius
$$
\mathrm{r}=0.529 Å
$$
Also, the angular momentum is quantised.
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi} \\
&2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\lambda \\
&\lambda=2 \pi \times 0.529 Å
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: If the kinetic energy of an electron is increased four times, the wavelength of the de-Broglie wave associated with it would become
question: If the kinetic energy of an electron is increased four times, the wavelength of the de-Broglie wave associated with it would become
solution:
de - Broglie wavelength is given by :
$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { K.E. }=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
& v^2=\frac{2 K E}{m} \\
& v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K E}{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$
Substituting this in equation (i)
$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{m} \sqrt{\frac{m}{2 K E}}
$$
$\lambda=h \sqrt{\frac{1}{2 m(\text { K.E. })}}$
i.e. $\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{K E}}$
$\therefore$ when $K E$ become 4 times wavelength become $1 / 2$.

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of radial node/s for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></math> orbital is:
question: The number of radial node/s for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></math> orbital is:
solution:
A point or plane of an orbital where the electron density of the electron of an orbital is zero is called a node. Nodes can be two types; one is a radial node and another one is an angular node.

Number of radial node=n-l-1

Now, for 3p orbital, the value of n is 3, and the azimuthal quantum number is 1. Therefore, the number of radial node is, n-l-1=(3-1-1)=1

Hence, ans will be A

subject: Chemistry
q: For electron in ' 2 s ' and ' 2 p ' orbitals, the orbital angular momentum values, respectively are :
question: For electron in ' 2 s ' and ' 2 p ' orbitals, the orbital angular momentum values, respectively are :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Orbital angular momentum }=\sqrt{\ell(\ell+1)} \frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { For } 2 \mathrm{~s} \text { orbital }: \ell=0 \\ & \text { Orbital angular momentum }=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { For } 2 \mathrm{p} \text { orbital }: \ell=1 \\ & \quad \begin{aligned} \text { Orbital angular momentum } & =\sqrt{1(1+2)} \frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi} \\ & =\sqrt{2} \frac{\mathrm{~h}}{2 \pi}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in shell with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> are:
question: Maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in shell with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> are:
solution:
The maximum number of electrons that could be present in a shell is given by the rule 2n2.

Where n is the shell number.

The first shell K shell can accommodate a maximum of 2 electrons (2 × 12 = 2).

The second shell L shell can accommodate a maximum of 8 electrons (2 × 22 = 8).

Likewise, the 4th shell named N can accommodate a maximum of 32 electrons (2 × 42 = 2 × 16 = 32).
